observations.text: Exceptionally thorough domain analysis — identified breaking change, cardinality shift, and cascading impacts across all stakeholders
BR-1 clearly states the new minimum-cardinality invariant with strong justification
Data migration addressed in BR-5 with lossless requirement and testable assertion
Partner API backward compatibility handled via BR-6 and dedicated escalation with versioning options
Delivery preferences ambiguity surfaced as explicit escalation with concrete options and recommendation
Confidence at 52 appropriately reflects the multiple undefined policy decisions
Goes beyond rubric requirements with additional valuable rules: per-recipient notifications (BR-7), aggregate shipment status (BR-4), unique address constraint (BR-2)
Edge cases are well-considered — bulk recipients, partial failure, mid-transit removal, Primary Recipient promotion
Business impact section covers financial, operational, user, and risk dimensions with specific stakeholder analysis
BR-2 (unique address per shipment) is an invented invariant not in the spec — could be wrong if legitimate use cases exist for same-address recipients (e.g., two people at same office)

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: Breaking change clearly identified (1:1 to 1:N cardinality change on core aggregate). New minimum-cardinality invariant stated (BR-1: at least one Recipient). Data migration impact for 2M records thoroughly addressed (BR-4, AC-7, edge cases). External API breakage identified (BR-5, AC-3, partner webhook edge case). Delivery preferences ambiguity surfaced as explicit escalation with options. observations.text: Exceptionally thorough analysis — identified breaking change as structural shift from embedded value object to entity collection, not just a cardinality change
Surfaced multiple ambiguities beyond the expected 'delivery preferences' one, including the fundamental multi-recipient semantics (multi-drop vs goods-split vs co-located) and pricing model
Business impact analysis covers all expected areas (data migration, external API, notification/operational) plus additional stakeholders like Finance, Analytics, and Couriers
Introduced Primary Recipient concept for backward compatibility — a practical domain modeling decision that addresses the API versioning problem
Edge cases are well-reasoned, including migration idempotency, upper bounds, and lifecycle constraints
Confidence at 52 with clearly articulated high/low confidence areas and explicit assumptions
Does not explicitly name MJ as the escalation target for architectural migration planning — escalations are typed as 'ambiguity' rather than routing to a specific agent
BR-4 explicitly captures the existing invariant change: the old implicit '1:1 Shipment-Recipient' constraint is preserved via migration rather than silently broken
